text: With trocars in place, the surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz at the base of the transverse mesocolon. The small bowel is measured and transected 40 centimeters from this point. A side-to-side jejunojejunostomy is created after measuring 20 centimeters for the Roux limb, with the anastomosis visibly forming between the bowel segments.

The surgeon closes the aneurotomy and then closes the mesenteric defect to prevent internal hernia formation at the site of the jejunojejunostomy. The roux limb, approximately twenty centimeters in length, is brought in a retrocolic fashion after exposing the pancreas and opening the pancreatic duct.

The surgeon opens the lesser sac at the greater curve of the stomach, detaching the greater omentum along its length to access the pancreas. The posterior wall of the stomach is visible, followed by the identification of the pancreas, which appears firm and inflamed due to chronic pancreatitis. A needle is used to withdraw clear fluid from the pancreatic duct.

The surgeon uses a hook cautery on the anterior wall of the pancreas to access the pancreatic duct, carrying the ductotomy down to the tail of the pancreas. A Maryland dissector is used to access the duct, revealing a wide channel as the duct is opened towards the tail.

The surgeon uses a hook cautery to incise the anterior wall of the pancreas, encountering difficulty due to the gland's firm texture. Subsequently, the roux limb is positioned in a retrocolic fashion, with its blind end directed towards the tail of the pancreas.

The surgeon examines the completed pancreatic ductotomy extending to the tail of the pancreas. An enterotomy is made in the roux limb, which is then extended to match the length of the pancreatic ductotomy.

The surgeon begins the anastomosis of the pancreatic duct to the small bowel using a running PDS suture. The suture is barbed, allowing it to lock the tissue in place as it is pulled through. The anastomosis starts at the tail of the pancreas and proceeds along the inferior border of the ductotomy.

The surgeon attempts to drive a needle through the firm and resistant pancreatic tissue, pulling the barbed suture taut to lock the small bowel against the pancreas. Due to a size mismatch, the pancreatic ductotomy is extended towards the head of the pancreas to align with the enterotomy length.

The surgeon continues with full-thickness bites through both the small bowel and the pancreatic wall, reaching the apex of the inferior wall of the pancreas. The suture is brought back through the small bowel, utilizing the barbed nature to lock it in place. The suture is cut to length and held up for orientation. Needle trauma is visible due to calcification within the pancreatic wall, highlighting the firm texture of the pancreas.

The surgeon performs the superior aspect of the anastomosis by running a second suture from the tail of the pancreas, attaching the small bowel to the pancreatic wall. The sutures are crossed at the apex to ensure overlap, and the barbed suture locks itself in place without the need for tying.
The surgeon tacks the roux limb to the transverse mesocolon, securing it to prevent potential internal hernias.
